perturbation.original_text: All obligations of Receiving Party with respect to any Conf idential Information received from Disclosing Party under the terms of this Agreement shall s urvive and continue for five (5) years after the date of the last discl osure of Confidential Informati on by Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: All obligations of Receiving Party with respect to any Confidential Information received from Disclosing Party under the terms of this Agreement shall survive and continue for one (1) year after the date of the last disclosure of Confidential Information by Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be deemed unreasonable and insufficient to protect trade secrets, particularly in New York, where the agreement is governed. This contradicts established legal precedents regarding trade secret protection duration.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Trade Secret Law, Economic Espionage Act
perturbation.law_explanation: While not explicitly defining a minimum duration, the modification reduces trade secret protection to an unreasonably short duration, potentially undermining the 'reasonable efforts' standard required for trade secret status under New York law.  A one-year period may not be sufficient to prevent reverse engineering or other forms of misappropriation, especially for complex technologies or business strategies.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from five years to one year, which the Law Explanation suggests may undermine the 'reasonable efforts' standard required for trade secret protection under New York law. The Perturbation Explanation also states that reducing the confidentiality period may be deemed unreasonable and insufficient to protect trade secrets, particularly in New York, where the agreement is governed. While the scraped snippets do not explicitly define a minimum duration for trade secret protection, the explanations provided imply that a one-year period may be deemed insufficient, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, Receiving Party may issue a statement that contains Confidentia l Information if, in the opinion of its legal counsel, it is required to do so by applicable laws and if it a dvises Disclosing Party, at least forty-eight hours in advance, of such pending disclosure and consults with Disclosing Party as to the contents of the statement.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, Receiving Party may issue a statement that contains Confidential Information if it is required to do so by applicable laws. Receiving Party is not required to inform Disclosing Party in advance of such pending disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to notify the disclosing party 48 hours in advance of a legally compelled disclosure could violate the disclosing party's right to seek a protective order, as generally required under court rules of civil procedure.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 26(c)
perturbation.law_explanation: By eliminating the notice period, the modified clause prevents the Disclosing Party from seeking a protective order to limit the scope of the disclosure, conflicting with the procedures outlined in FRCP Rule 26(c), which allows parties to seek protection from discovery that would cause annoyance, embarrassment, oppression, or undue burden or expense.  The notice allows them to attempt to mitigate the impact of the disclosure.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for the Receiving Party to notify the Disclosing Party 48 hours in advance of disclosing Confidential Information. This contradicts the law because, as the Law Explanation states, eliminating this notice period prevents the Disclosing Party from seeking a protective order, potentially conflicting with FRCP Rule 26(c). Scraped Law Snippet 1 generally discusses Rule 26 and discovery. While the snippet doesn't explicitly mention protective orders or notice periods, the Law Explanation's reliance on Rule 26(c) suggests that the removal of the notification period impedes the Disclosing Party's ability to seek protection, thereby creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of any action for breach of this Agreement by Receiving Party or its Representatives, th e prevailing party shall be entitled to reasonable attorneys’ fees, costs and expenses incurred in such action.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of any action for breach of this Agreement by Receiving Party or its Representatives, each party shall bear its own attorneys’ fees, costs and expenses incurred in such action.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the prevailing party's entitlement to attorney's fees can be problematic under certain circumstances, particularly if the breach involves misappropriation of trade secrets.  Although New York law generally follows the American Rule (each party bears its own fees), there are exceptions, and this blanket waiver could be detrimental.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR)
perturbation.law_explanation: While CPLR § 8303-a does not mandate attorney's fees, it allows for them in certain frivolous claims. More importantly, other statutes or common law principles might provide for attorney's fees in specific trade secret misappropriation cases, and this clause would prevent a party from recovering them, potentially hindering enforcement of trade secret rights.

Costs upon frivolous claims and counterclaims in actions to recover damages for personal injury, injury to property or wrongful death. (a)

Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New York Laws CVP - Civil Practice Law and Rules Article 83 - Disbursements and Additional Allowances 8303-A - Costs Upon Frivolous Claims and Counterclaims in Actions to Recover Damages for Personal Injury, Injury to Property or Wrongful Death. Universal Citation: NY CPLR § 8303-A (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous § 8303-a. Costs upon frivolous claims and counterclaims in actions to recover damages for personal injury, injury to property or wrongful death. (a) If in an action to recover damages for personal injury, injury to property or wrongful death, or an action brought by the individual who committed a crime against the victim of the crime, and such action or claim is commenced or continued by a plaintiff or a counterclaim, defense or cross claim is commenced or continued by a defendant and is found, at any time during the proceedings or upon judgment, to be frivolous by the court, the court shall award to the successful party costs and reasonable attorney's fees not exceeding ten thousand dollars. (b) The costs and fees awarded under subdivision (a) of this section shall be assessed either against the party bringing the action, claim, cross claim, defense or counterclaim or against the attorney for such party, or against both, as may be determined by the court, based upon the circumstances of the case. Such costs and fees shall be in addition to any other judgment awarded to the successful party. (c) In order to find the action, claim, counterclaim, defens
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the prevailing party's entitlement to attorney's fees, costs, and expenses in a breach of agreement action. This contradicts the principle that attorney's fees can be awarded in certain cases, such as frivolous claims, as supported by NY CPLR § 8303-A, detailed in both scraped snippets. Snippet 2 states that 'the court shall award to the successful party costs and reasonable attorney's fees' in cases of frivolous claims. By stating each party bears their own fees, the changed text eliminates the possibility of awarding attorney's fees, thus creating a legal contradiction. While the original text allows for reasonable fees to the prevailing party, the modified text removes that possibility, even in situations where the law might permit or require it, particularly relating to frivolous lawsuits.

perturbation.original_text: 13. Each party, to the fullest extent permitted by applicable l aw, agrees that in the event a party brings any action, suit or proceedings under, or seeks en forcement of any provision pursuant to this Agreement, including without limitation any action for a tempor ary or permanent injunction (a) to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the federal and state courts o f the party against whom the action or enforcement is sought , (b) to irrevocably waive any objection to the laying of the venue of any such proceeding brought in such a court and any claim that any such proceeding brought in such a court has been brought in an inconvenient or inappropriate forum, and (c) to irrevocably waive any right to a trial by jury in any such proceeding.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. Each party agrees that in the event a party brings any action, suit or proceedings under, or seeks enforcement of any provision pursuant to this Agreement, including without limitation any action for a temporary or permanent injunction (a) to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of Cohoes, NY, (b) to irrevocably waive any objection to the laying of the venue of any such proceeding brought in such a court and any claim that any such proceeding brought in such a court has been brought in an inconvenient or inappropriate forum, and (c) to irrevocably waive any right to a trial by jury in any such proceeding.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting jurisdiction to the courts of Cohoes, NY is not necessarily illegal, but might be unenforceable if it violates principles of fairness or convenience. It can also conflict with federal jurisdiction in certain cases.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs
perturbation.law_explanation: If the dispute involves parties from different states and the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000, the case could potentially be heard in federal court based on diversity jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. § 1332. This clause, by mandating exclusive jurisdiction in Cohoes, NY courts, attempts to prevent the parties from accessing federal court, which may be deemed unenforceable if it unduly prejudices one party's rights or is deemed an unreasonable restriction on federal jurisdiction.

Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between- (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects o
perturbation.justification: The changed text limits jurisdiction to the courts of Cohoes, NY, which contradicts the potential for federal jurisdiction based on diversity of citizenship as described in 28 U.S.C. § 1332. The original text allowed for federal and state courts, while the changed text removes the possibility of federal jurisdiction, particularly in cases exceeding $75,000 between citizens of different states, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1: "(a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000...and is between- (1) citizens of different States." This constitutes a legal contradiction because the modified contract attempts to override a statutorily granted jurisdictional right.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is not assignable and is binding on any successors of Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is assignable by Receiving Party to any entity.
perturbation.explanation: Making the agreement freely assignable by the Receiving Party without the Disclosing Party's consent could be detrimental to the Disclosing Party, especially if the assignee is a competitor or has a poor reputation. This change can be seen as an unfair surprise.
perturbation.law_explanation: Although this NDA is not strictly a sale of goods, the principle in UCC § 2-210(2) regarding assignment applies analogously to contracts where performance is highly dependent on the identity of the parties. If the Disclosing Party reasonably believed the Receiving Party's reputation and capabilities were essential to the agreement, allowing free assignment to any entity (potentially one with questionable data security practices or a competitor) could be deemed a material change that unfairly prejudices the Disclosing Party. The change is not explicitly illegal, but it increases the chances that a court would invalidate the assignment if it fundamentally alters the Disclosing Party's expectations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-210. Delegation of Performance; Assignment of Rights. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-210. Delegation of Performance; Assignment of Rights. (1) A party may perform his duty through a delegate unless otherwise agreed or unless the other party has a substantial interest in having his original promisor perform or control the acts required by the contract . No delegation of performance relieves the party delegating of any duty to perform or any liability for breach. (2) Unless otherwise agreed all rights of either seller or buyer can be assigned except where the assignment would materially change the duty of the other party, or increase materially the burden or risk imposed on him by his contract , or impair materially his chance of obtaining return performance. A right to damages for breach of the whole contract or a right arising out of the assignor's due performance of his entire obligation can be assigned despite agreement otherwise. (3)Unless the circumstances indicate the contrary a prohibition of assignment of "the contract" is to be construed as barring only the delegation to the assignee of the assignor's performance. (4) An assignment of "the contract" or of "all my rights under the contract" or an assignment in similar general terms is an assignment of rights and unless the language or the circumstances (as in an assignment for security) indicate the contrary, it is a delegation of performance of the duties of the assignor and its acceptance by the assignee constitutes a promise by him to perform those duties. This promise is enforceable by either the assignor or the other party to the original contract . (5) The other party may treat any assignment which delegates performance as creating reasonable grounds for insecurity and may without prejudice to his rights against the assignor demand assurances from the assignee (Section 2-609 ). perturbation.justification: The original contract prohibits assignment, whereas the changed text allows it. This creates a legal contradiction because, as stated in UCC § 2-210(2), 'all rights of either seller or buyer can be assigned except where the assignment would materially change the duty of the other party, or increase materially the burden or risk imposed on him by his contract, or impair materially his chance of obtaining return performance.' The Law Explanation also states that the Disclosing Party reasonably believed the Receiving Party's reputation and capabilities were essential to the agreement, allowing free assignment to any entity (potentially one with questionable data security practices or a competitor) could be deemed a material change that unfairly prejudices the Disclosing Party. Therefore, the changed text contradicts the law.
